focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The F train doors folded shut behind Marisol and smeared her reflection into the dark glass: coat collar wet at the neck, hair still flat from the hat she had shoved into her tote, eyes too bright for someone on day three. She stood with her hand around the center pole and counted stations the way she counted minutes in meetings, an old trick Luis had taught her for cravings and panic and any other kind of hunger that lied about urgency. There were seven stops left, then six, then five. She had texted Ada that she would bring dinner, then spent eleven dollars she could not spare on pork dumplings and two bottles of yuzu seltzer because she was not going to walk into Ada's apartment with wine, not even as a joke, not even sealed.

Outside the station the wind came off the avenue and slid under her sleeves. Sunset Park looked rinsed clean and mean in winter: halal carts wrapped in steam, nail salons still lit past nine, men smoking in front of shuttered storefronts while a delivery bike rattled by with a milk crate tied down by old bungee cords. She climbed Ada's block with her shoulders up by her ears, fighting the urge to check her phone for unread messages from work, from Luis, from nobody. Her account balance had dipped below three digits after the workshop penalty and rent transfer, and she could feel that fact as a second heartbeat every time she touched her wallet. She wanted tonight to be one room with one person and food that tasted like anything besides burnt coffee from church basements.

Ada opened the door in gray sweatpants, one sock on and one bare foot planted on the hardwood because she had been painting her toenails and forgotten to finish. A bottle of dark green polish sat on the floor beside her, cap off, brush balanced across the mouth li...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...en glass.

She lay down fully dressed except for shoes and pulled the blanket over her shoulders. Behind her closed eyes she saw Gloria in the parking lot, peppermint on her breath, saying both can be true. Protection and manipulation in the same room. That line had the shape of scripture, and Marisol hated that it might also be accurate.

At one-thirty she lurched to the sink and vomited water, bile, and a sour ghost of vodka that burned her nose on the way out. Her ribs cramped. Tears jumped out involuntary. She rinsed her mouth and sat on the bathroom floor with her back against the tub, forehead on her knees, listening to the radiator click like an impatient metronome.

She thought of Tomas, not saint or junkie, just a body in a motel room she could not picture without filling in details from cheap TV crime shows. She thought of Gloria kneeling by a sink with no gloves. She thought of herself at nine, carrying a cardboard halo into school with glue still tacky on her fingers.

She had wanted one direct confrontation before institutional spin reset the story. She got it. She got the truth and still could not say which part of it belonged to whom.

At two she forced herself up, drank more water, and wrote four lines on an index card with a felt-tip pen that bled through.

I drank tonight.

I lied to Luis on the phone.

I lied by omission to Ada.

I'm still here.

She taped the card to the lamp base where she would see it on waking. The last line looked fake and necessary at once.

Before sleep took her, she opened Messages one final time and reread the unsent silence in Luis's thread. No confession, no correction, only his last practical question.

Where are you?

She locked the phone and slid it under the pillow as if pressure could keep truth in place until morning.
